# Imajin Network (MJN Protocol)
# llms-full.txt — Complete technical reference for AI consumption
# v0.6 · May 2026 · DRAFT
# Ryan Veteze · ryan@imajin.ai · github.com/ima-jin/imajin-ai
#
# This document is intended as a complete integration reference.
# An LLM should be able to build a working client from this document alone.

---

## 1. What This Is

MJN is a cryptographic operating system that runs as a single consolidated kernel with a federated userspace.

The **kernel** (`apps/kernel`) is one Next.js application handling identity, authentication, payments, messaging, media, trust graph, and registry. One domain, one cookie, one build. The userspace apps (events, market, learn, coffee, links, dykil) are independent Next.js apps that consume the kernel via HTTP, each with their own schema, each replaceable.

No service mesh. No orchestration framework. No API gateway. Kernel routes behind Caddy. Userspace apps behind Caddy. The value is in the cryptographic substrate underneath — signed identity chains that make every action verifiable and every relationship bilateral.

**Build stats (May 2026):**
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Monorepo version | 0.6.1 |
| Lines of TypeScript/TSX | ~134,000 |
| Commits | 2,467 |
| Days building | ~110 (started Feb 1, 2026) |
| Shared packages | 20 |

---

## 2. Architecture: Kernel + Bus + Vault + Broker

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Userspace Apps (federated, replaceable)                    │
│  events · market · learn · coffee · links · dykil           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Kernel (single Next.js app, single domain)                 │
│  auth · pay · chat · media · profile · connections · registry│
│  ├─ Event Bus (@imajin/bus) — reactor chains                │
│  ├─ Vault (imajin-cli) — encrypted key-value store          │
│  └─ Broker (specced) — consent-gated selective disclosure   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  DFOS Proof Chains (L0–L5) · .fair Attribution              │
│  Ed25519 · CID · dag-cbor · countersignatures               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Why this works:**
- The kernel is sovereign infrastructure. One cookie, one session, no CORS between auth/pay/chat.
- Userspace apps inherit the full trust graph and identity system by importing `@imajin/auth`.
- The complexity lives in the chain layer, not the orchestration layer.
- Runs on a single server with pm2. No Kubernetes. No Docker in production.

### Kernel Consolidation

The kernel consolidated what were formerly 8 separate services into one application:

| Route prefix | Responsibility |
|--------------|----------------|
| `/auth/*` | DIDs, sessions, attestations, challenges, registration, agents, apps |
| `/pay/*` | Stripe checkout, settlement, .fair splits, MJNx ledger |
| `/chat/*` | DID-based messaging, WebSocket tokens, conversations |
| `/media/*` | Asset storage, .fair sidecars, on-the-fly resizing |
| `/profile/*` | Public profiles, handles, presence |
| `/connections/*` | Trust graph, pods, invites |
| `/registry/*` | Node federation, app manifests, DFOS relay |
| `/input/*` | Voice transcription, file processing |

Chat's WebSocket server (`ws-server.js`) runs as a separate process. Caddy routes `/chat/ws` to it.

---

## 3. Identity

### DID Format

- **Soft DID**: `did:imajin:<nanoid(44)>` — random stable identifier minted at email verification. No keypair. Cannot sign. Stored in DB with a placeholder key.
- **Hard DID**: `did:imajin:<sha256(Ed25519_pubkey)>` — deterministically derived from the Ed25519 public key. Cryptographically self-certifying. Can sign attestations, messages, etc.

Conversation DIDs (chat):
- DM: `did:imajin:dm:<sha256(sorted_member_dids)>.slice(0,16)` — deterministic from participants
- Group: `did:imajin:group:<sha256(sorted_members).slice(0,16)>` — deterministic from sorted member DIDs
- Event chat: `did:imajin:event:<eventId>` or `did:imajin:evt_<eventId>`

### Five Standing Tiers

Tier progression: `soft → preliminary → established → steward → operator`

- **soft**: Email-verified only. No keypair. Can buy tickets, enroll in courses. Cannot send messages, sign attestations, or create listings.
- **preliminary**: Ed25519 keypair + invite code. Full trust graph access. Can send messages, create events and listings, issue attestations.
- **established**: Earned through attestation history. Can vouch for others, invite new members.
- **steward**: Trusted community steward. Elevated governance rights.
- **operator**: Platform operator. Full access.

Helper functions from `@imajin/auth`:
```typescript
import { isVerifiedTier, isEstablishedTier, isStewardTier, isOperatorTier } from '@imajin/auth';
// isVerifiedTier: preliminary+   isEstablishedTier: established+
// isStewardTier: steward+        isOperatorTier: operator only
```

### Four Identity Scopes

- **actor**: One DID, one keypair. Subtypes: `human`, `agent`, `device`, `presence`, `org`, `service`, `event`.
- **family**: Intimate shared trust. Shared resources, delegated authority.
- **community**: Shared practice. Quorum-governed, contribution-weighted.
- **business**: Structured entity. Roles, delegation chains. Cannot initiate connections — all outreach is consent-gated.

### identity_members Table

Group identities use a membership table (`identity_members`) linking a group DID to member DIDs with roles:

```typescript
// From apps/kernel/src/db/schemas/auth.ts (excerpt)
export const identityMembers = authSchema.table('identity_members', {
  identityDid: text('identity_did').notNull(),   // group DID
  memberDid: text('member_did').notNull(),       // actor DID
  role: text('role').notNull().default('member'), // owner | admin | maintainer | agent | member
  addedBy: text('added_by'),
  addedAt: timestamp('added_at').defaultNow(),
  removedAt: timestamp('removed_at'),
});
```

### Act-As Delegation

Two delegation mechanisms:

1. **X-Acting-As** — group identity impersonation. A controller (owner/admin/maintainer/agent) of a group DID can act on its behalf:
```typescript
// In requireAuth — validated server-side
const actingAs = request.headers.get("x-acting-as");
// Calls: GET /auth/api/groups/{groupDid}/controllers/{callerDid}
// Returns: { valid: true, role: 'admin', allowedServices: ['events', 'market'] }
```

2. **X-Acting-For** — agent delegation. An agent DID can act for its principal:
```typescript
const actingFor = request.headers.get("x-acting-for");
// Validates that caller has role='agent' for the target identity
```

### Soft DID Creation (Lazy Minting)

DIDs are minted on first use. No batch import, no onboarding step.

```typescript
// Server-side: email verification creates a soft DID
POST /auth/api/onboard
{ "email": "user@example.com", "name": "Jane", "redirectUrl": "...", "context": "buy a ticket" }

// Returns: { sent: true }
// User clicks email link → GET /auth/api/onboard/verify?token=...
// → soft DID created, session cookie set, redirect to redirectUrl
```

### DFOS Anchoring

Every `did:imajin` can be anchored to a DFOS proof chain. Same Ed25519 keypair produces both `did:imajin:xxx` and `did:dfos:xxx` — byte-compatible, same curve.

```typescript
import { createIdentityChain, verifyChain, DFOS_DID_PREFIX } from '@imajin/dfos';

// Create a DFOS chain from an Imajin keypair
const { did, log, operationCID } = await createIdentityChain({
  privateKeyHex: '64-char-hex...',
  publicKeyHex: '64-char-hex...',
});
// Returns: did = 'did:dfos:...', log = [jwsToken]

// Verify a chain
const verified = await verifyChain(log);
// Returns: VerifiedIdentity with did, authKeys, assertKeys, controllerKeys
```

Chain verification endpoint:
```
GET /auth/api/identity/{did}/verify
→ { hasDfosChain: true, chainValid: true, chainLength: 3, dbConsistent: true }
```

---

## 4. Authentication

All auth flows produce the same session cookie (`imajin_session` in prod, `imajin_session_dev` in dev). Cookie attributes: `HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Domain=.imajin.ai` — valid across all `*.imajin.ai` services.

### Flow 1: Soft DID (Email / Magic Link)

**Direct soft session:**
```
POST /auth/api/session/soft
{ "email": "user@example.com", "name": "Jane" }
→ { "did": "did:imajin:abc...", "handle": null, "type": "human", "name": "Jane", "tier": "soft" }
```

**OnboardGate (email verification with proof):**
```
POST /auth/api/onboard
{ "email": "user@example.com", "name": "Jane", "redirectUrl": "...", "context": "buy a ticket" }
→ { "sent": true }
```
Token TTL: 15 minutes. Grace window for email scanners: 60 seconds.

**Magic link re-auth (soft DIDs only):**
```
POST /auth/api/onboard
{ "email": "user@example.com", "mode": "login", "wantPolling": true }
→ { "sent": true, "pollHandle": "..." }
```
Poll: `GET /auth/api/onboard/poll?handle=...` → claim via `POST /auth/api/onboard/claim`

### Flow 2: Challenge-Response (Ed25519 Keypair)

```
POST /auth/api/challenge
{ "handle": "alice" }  // or { "did": "did:imajin:..." }
→ { "challengeId": "chl_xxx", "challenge": "hex...", "expiresAt": "..." }
```
Challenge TTL: 5 minutes.

```
POST /auth/api/authenticate
{ "challengeId": "chl_xxx", "signature": "128-char-hex-ed25519-sig", "dfosChain": { ... } }
→ { "did": "did:imajin:...", "handle": "alice", "type": "human", "name": "Alice" }
```
Signature: Ed25519 sign of the raw `challenge` hex string using your private key.

### Flow 3: Registration (New Keypair)

Derive DID client-side:
```typescript
const did = "did:imajin:" + sha256(publicKey_bytes).hex();
```

```
POST /auth/api/register
{
  "publicKey": "64-char-hex...",
  "handle": "alice",
  "name": "Alice",
  "type": "human",
  "signature": "128-char-hex...",
  "inviteCode": "abc123...",
  "email": "alice@example.com",
  "dfosChain": { ... }
}
→ { "did": "did:imajin:...", "handle": "alice", "type": "human", "created": true }
```

Type values: `human | agent | presence | org | device | service | event`
Human registration requires an invite code. Service accounts bypass the invite gate.

### Auth Middleware Hierarchy

`@imajin/auth` exposes these guards:

| Function | Tier accepted | Use case |
|----------|--------------|----------|
| `requireAuth(request, options?)` | soft+ | Most endpoints |
| `requireHardDID(request)` | preliminary+ | Signing, trust-gated actions |
| `requireEstablishedDID(request)` | established+ | Vouching, advanced trust ops |

All return `{ identity: Identity } | { error: string, status: number }`.

`Identity` shape:
```typescript
interface Identity {
  id: string;                    // DID
  scope: string;                 // 'actor' | 'family' | 'community' | 'business'
  subtype?: string;              // 'human' | 'agent' | 'device' | etc.
  name?: string;
  handle?: string;
  email?: string | null;
  tier?: "soft" | "preliminary" | "established" | "steward" | "operator";
  chainVerified?: boolean;
  actingAs?: string;             // group DID being impersonated
  actingAsRole?: string;         // role in that group
  actingAsServices?: string[];   // restricted service list (undefined = full)
  actingFor?: string;            // DID the agent is delegated to
  actingForRole?: string;        // always 'agent' for now
}
```

In API routes:
```typescript
const authResult = await requireAuth(request, { verifyChain: true, service: 'events' });
if ('error' in authResult) {
  return NextResponse.json({ error: authResult.error }, { status: authResult.status });
}
const { identity } = authResult;
```

### App Authentication (Federated Services)

Userspace apps authenticate via `requireAppAuth()` using attestation-based consent:

```typescript
// Client sends:
X-App-DID: did:imajin:app_xxx
X-App-Authorization: att_xxx

// Server validates:
const result = await requireAppAuth(request, { scope: 'events:read' });
if ('error' in result) { /* 401/403 */ }
const { appDid, userDid, scopes, attestationId } = result.appAuth;
```

The attestation must be type `app.authorized`, issued by the user, subject = app DID, payload containing approved scopes. Validated at `/auth/api/apps/validate` (internal endpoint, Bearer `ATTESTATION_INTERNAL_API_KEY`).

### Session Validation

```
GET /auth/api/session  (with Cookie: imajin_session=...)
→ { did, handle, type, name, scope, subtype, tier, chainVerified }
```

Server components use `getSession()` from `@imajin/auth`:
```typescript
import { getSession } from '@imajin/auth';
const identity = await getSession({ service: 'events' });
// Reads from next/headers cookie store, validates acting-as server-side
```

### Bearer Tokens (Machine Clients)

```
Authorization: Bearer <token>
```

Validated via `POST /auth/api/validate`:
```
POST /auth/api/validate
{ "token": "imajin_tok_xxx" }
→ { valid: true, identity: { id, type, name, ... } }
```

---

## 5. Event Bus

The event bus (`@imajin/bus`) is the nervous system. Services publish events; reactor chains handle side effects.

### Core Pattern

```typescript
import { publish } from '@imajin/bus';

await publish('listing.purchased', {
  issuer: buyerDid,
  subject: sellerDid,
  scope: 'market',
  payload: {
    context_id: listingId,
    context_type: 'market',
    amount: 15000,               // cents
    currency: 'CAD',
    fairManifest: { ... },       // .fair fee manifest
    funded: true,
    funded_provider: 'stripe',
    buyerDid,
    metadata: { listingId },
    interestDids: [buyerDid, sellerDid],
  },
});
```

The bus runs a reactor chain for the event type. Each reactor is fire-and-forget by default (`await: false`), or blocking (`await: true`) for ordering-sensitive operations like settlement.

### BusEvent Shape

```typescript
interface BusEvent {
  type: string;              // 'listing.purchased', 'identity.created', etc.
  issuer: string;            // DID of the actor
  subject: string;           // DID of the target
  scope: string;             // service scope ('events', 'pay', 'market', etc.)
  payload?: Record<string, unknown>;
  correlationId?: string;    // trace across reactors
  timestamp?: string;        // ISO 8601, defaults to now
}
```

### Reactor Chain Configuration

Phase 1 uses hardcoded defaults in `packages/bus/src/config.ts`. Phase 2 will be DB-backed.

```typescript
// Example: listing.purchased chain
{
  'listing.purchased': [
    { type: 'attestation', config: { attestationType: 'listing.purchased' }, enabled: true },
    { type: 'mjn',       config: { attestationType: 'listing.purchased' }, enabled: true },
    { type: 'settle',    config: {}, await: true, enabled: true },
    { type: 'notify',    config: { scope: 'market:purchase' }, enabled: true },
  ],
}
```

Built-in reactors:
| Reactor | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `attestation` | Issues a signed attestation via auth service |
| `mjn` | Credits MJN tokens based on emission schedule |
| `settle` | Calls pay service to process .fair settlement |
| `notify` | Sends notification + interest signals |
| `emit` | Emits structured log/event for analytics |
| `dfos` | Phase 2: direct DFOS chain append (currently deferred to attestation path) |
| `webhook` | POSTs event payload to configured external URL |

### The listing.purchased Flow (Cleanest Example)

```typescript
// apps/market/app/api/webhook/route.ts
// Called by pay service after Stripe payment succeeds

bus.publish('listing.purchased', {
  issuer: body.metadata?.buyerDid || '',
  subject: listing.sellerDid,
  scope: 'market',
  payload: {
    context_id: listingId,
    context_type: 'market',
    amount: body.metadata?.amount || 0,
    currency: body.metadata?.currency || 'CAD',
    fairManifest: listing.fairManifest || null,
    funded: true,
    funded_provider: 'stripe',
    buyerDid: body.metadata?.buyerDid || '',
    metadata: { listingId },
    interestDids: [buyerDid, sellerDid].filter(Boolean),
  },
});
```

Chain execution:
1. **attestation reactor** → `emitAttestation({ issuer_did: buyer, subject_did: seller, type: 'listing.purchased', ... })`
2. **mjn reactor** → looks up `EMISSION_SCHEDULE['listing.purchased']`, credits buyer 0.25% of sale and seller 0.25% of sale in MJN
3. **settle reactor** → resolves .fair chain, deducts Stripe processing fee from seller share, POSTs to pay service `/api/settle`
4. **notify reactor** → sends `market:purchase` notification to seller, emits interest signals to both DIDs

### Reactor Registry

```typescript
import { registerReactor } from '@imajin/bus';

registerReactor('my-reactor', async (event, config) => {
  // Your side-effect logic
});
```

### Type-Safe Events

```typescript
import type { BusEventMap, BusEventType } from '@imajin/bus';

// Payloads are typed — the compiler catches bad call sites
await publish('identity.created', {
  issuer: did, subject: did, scope: 'auth',
  payload: { scope: 'actor', subtype: 'human', tier: 'soft', context_id: did, context_type: 'identity' }
});
```

---

## 6. Vault

The vault is an encrypted key-value store. It lives in the `imajin-cli` repo (separate from the monorepo) and is used by node operators for secrets management.

### Cryptography

- **Ed25519 → X25519 key derivation** using `@noble/curves/ed25519` Montgomery conversion
- **XSalsa20-Poly1305** authenticated encryption via `tweetnacl` (NaCl box/secretbox)
- **CID addressing** — every encrypted blob gets a deterministic `dag-cbor + SHA-256` CID

```typescript
import {
  encrypt, decrypt, serializeBlob, deserializeBlob, computeCid,
  ed25519ToX25519Keys, ed25519PublicToX25519
} from '@imajin/cli/crypto/vault-crypto';

// Encrypt a secret to a recipient's Ed25519 public key
const encryptedBlob = encrypt(plaintext, recipientEd25519PublicKeyHex, senderEd25519SecretKeyHex);
const serialized = serializeBlob(encryptedBlob);
const cid = await computeCid(serialized);

// Decrypt
const blob = deserializeBlob(serialized);
const plaintext = decrypt(blob, senderEd25519PublicKeyHex, recipientEd25519SecretKeyHex);
```

### CLI Interface

```bash
imajin vault set <KEY> <VALUE>     # Encrypt and store
imajin vault get <KEY>             # Retrieve and decrypt
imajin vault list                  # List all entries
```

Storage: `~/.imajin/vault.json`. Each entry tracks field, CID, encrypted blob, nonce, sender DID, sender pubkey, timestamp, and `previousCid` for history chaining.

### Bus Events for Hot-Reload

Vault changes can emit bus events (specced) so kernel services hot-reload secrets without restart.

---

## 7. Selective Disclosure & Broker

The broker is a consent-gated data release layer. Specced in #786/#1003. Not yet implemented in the monorepo — described here for integration planning.

### Core Pattern

A subject's personal data is encrypted at rest. Only released to specific requesters when the subject has granted consent for a specific purpose — with every release audited.

### Consent Grants

```json
{
  "dietary_restrictions": [
    { "purpose": "restaurant_reservation", "release": "raw", "duration": "trip" }
  ],
  "allergies": [
    { "purpose": "restaurant_reservation", "release": "attestation" },
    { "purpose": "emergency_medical", "release": "raw", "duration": "permanent" }
  ]
}
```

### Release Modes

| Mode | What requester sees |
|------|---------------------|
| **raw** | Actual value: "vegetarian, gluten-free" |
| **attestation** | Confirmation without details: `{ has_allergies: true }` |
| **none** | Field omitted from response |

### Request Flow

```typescript
// Specced API — not yet live
bus.broker({
  requester: "did:tripian:restaurant_abc",
  subject: travelerDid,
  fields: ["dietary", "allergies", "budget"],
  purpose: "restaurant_reservation"
});
// Returns filtered envelope + writes audit log
```

### Shadow Mode (PoC)

For the Hawaii PoC, the broker runs in shadow mode: consent checks and field filtering execute and log decisions, but nothing is gated — the app continues to work. After PoC, shadow mode flips to enforcement.

### Integration Pattern (Tripian)

1. **Get-or-create DID** — first time any entity is referenced, call `POST /registry/identity`
2. **Set traveler preferences** — encrypt and store via vault API, keyed to traveler DID
3. **Request data** — call `broker.request()` with provider DID, traveler DID, fields, purpose
4. **Receive filtered response** — only consented fields, in appropriate release mode

Zero migration required. Tripian's existing systems don't change. The DID layer wraps around them.

---

## 8. Attestations

Attestations are cryptographically signed claims about identities. Unsigned attestations are rejected at write.

### Shape

```typescript
interface Attestation {
  id: string;                    // att_xxx
  issuerDid: string;
  subjectDid: string;
  type: string;                  // from ATTESTATION_TYPES
  contextId: string | null;      // e.g. event DID
  contextType: string | null;    // e.g. 'event'
  payload: Record<string, unknown> | null;
  signature: string;             // Ed25519 hex (128 chars) — legacy
  cid: string | null;            // dag-cbor CID of payload
  authorJws: string | null;      // JWS compact token (author sig)
  witnessJws: string | null;     // JWS compact token (countersignature)
  attestationStatus: string;     // 'pending' | 'bilateral' | 'declined' | 'collecting' | 'executed' | 'expired'
  documentHash: string | null;
  documentAssetId: string | null;
  totalSigners: number | null;
  issuedAt: Date;
  expiresAt: Date | null;
  revokedAt: Date | null;
}
```

### Attestation Types (Live)

```typescript
const ATTESTATION_TYPES = [
  'transaction.settled', 'customer', 'connection.invited', 'connection.accepted',
  'vouch', 'session.created', 'identity.created', 'identity.verified.preliminary',
  'identity.verified.hard', 'identity.verified.steward', 'identity.verified.operator',
  'tip.granted', 'ticket.purchased', 'listing.purchased', 'group.created',
  'group.member.added', 'group.member.removed', 'group.member.left',
  'pod.created', 'pod.member.added', 'pod.member.removed', 'pod.role.changed',
  'scope.onboard', 'event.created', 'event.attendance', 'handle.claimed',
  'learn.enrolled', 'learn.completed', 'stub.created', 'asset.fair.upgraded',
  'document.created', 'document.signed', 'document.executed', 'document.declined',
  'app.authorized',
] as const;
```

### Signing

Sign the canonicalized JSON of:
```json
{
  "subject_did": "did:imajin:subject...",
  "type": "connection.invited",
  "context_id": "inv_abc123",
  "context_type": "connection",
  "payload": { "delivery": "link" },
  "issued_at": 1742900000000
}
```

`canonicalize()` sorts keys deterministically. `issued_at` is Unix milliseconds. Result is 128-char hex Ed25519 signature.

### Creating an Attestation

```
POST /auth/api/attestations
Cookie: imajin_session=...
{
  "issuer_did": "did:imajin:issuer...",
  "subject_did": "did:imajin:subject...",
  "type": "vouch",
  "context_id": "inv_abc123",
  "context_type": "connection",
  "payload": { "note": "great colleague" },
  "signature": "deadbeef...128-char-hex...",
  "issued_at": 1742900000000
}
```

Internal (server-side) attestations are signed by the platform keypair (`AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY`):
```
POST /auth/api/attestations/internal
Authorization: Bearer {ATTESTATION_INTERNAL_API_KEY}
{ issuer_did, subject_did, type, context_id?, context_type?, payload? }
```

### Querying

```
GET /auth/api/attestations?subject_did={did}&type=vouch&limit=20
GET /auth/api/attestations/{did}   // shorthand
```

### Countersigning

```
POST /auth/api/attestations/countersign
{ "attestationId": "att_abc", "witnessJws": "<jws>" }
```
Transitions status from `pending` → `bilateral`. Only the subject can countersign.

---

## 9. .fair Attribution

Every asset carries a `.fair` manifest: who contributed, in what proportion, under what terms.

### Manifest Format (v1.1)

```typescript
interface FairManifestV1_1 {
  fair: "1.1";
  version: "1.1";
  id: string;
  type: string;
  owner: string;               // DID
  created: string;             // ISO 8601
  source?: string;
  access: FairAccessV1_1 | "public" | "private";
  transfer?: FairTransferV1_1;
  fees?: FairFee[];
  attribution: DidShareList;   // who gets paid
  distribution?: {             // usage rights
    reproduction?: FairDistributionRight;
    streaming?: FairDistributionRight;
    derivative?: FairDistributionRight;
    syndication?: FairDistributionRight;
  };
  training?: FairTraining;
  commercial?: FairCommercial;
  integrity?: FairIntegrity;
  terms?: string;
  intent?: FairIntent;
  signature?: Signature;       // Ed25519, base64url
  tipping?: { enabled: boolean };
  settlement?: SettlementConfig;
  chain?: DidShareList;        // backward compat alias
  distributions?: DidShareList; // backward compat alias
}
```

### Fee Cascade

```typescript
import { buildFairManifest } from '@imajin/fair';

const manifest = buildFairManifest({
  creatorDid: "did:imajin:alice...",
  contentDid: "did:imajin:asset_xxx",
  contentType: "track",
  scopeDid: null,
  nodeOperatorDid: "did:imajin:node...",
  nodeFeeBps: 50,          // 0.5% — clamped to [25, 200]
  buyerCreditBps: 25,      // 0.25% — clamped to [25, 200]
});
```

Fee cascade order:
1. **Protocol fee**: fixed at 1.0% (`PROTOCOL_FEE_BPS = 100`)
2. **Node fee**: operator-configurable, default 0.5%, bounds [0.25%, 2.0%]
3. **Buyer credit**: operator-configurable, default 0.25%, bounds [0.25%, 2.0%]
4. **Scope fee**: optional, only when `scopeDid` + `scopeFeeBps` provided
5. **Seller share**: remainder

Chain placeholder DIDs:
- `BUYER_PLACEHOLDER` → resolved to actual buyer DID at settlement
- `NODE_PLACEHOLDER` → resolved to `NODE_DID` env var

### Settlement via Bus Reactor

The `settleReactor` (`packages/bus/src/reactors/settle.ts`):
1. Extracts amount, currency, fairManifest from event payload
2. Resolves placeholder DIDs in the chain
3. Deducts estimated Stripe processing fee from seller share
4. Fixes rounding drift by adjusting the seller entry
5. POSTs to pay service `/api/settle` with resolved chain

```typescript
// The settle reactor POSTs this body to pay service:
{
  from_did: buyerDid,
  total_amount: 14.63,        // dollars after fee deduction
  service: "market",
  type: "listing.purchased",
  funded: true,
  funded_provider: "stripe",
  fair_manifest: {
    chain: [
      { did: "did:imajin:protocol...", amount: 0.15, role: "protocol" },
      { did: "did:imajin:node...", amount: 0.07, role: "node" },
      { did: "did:imajin:buyer...", amount: 0.04, role: "buyer_credit" },
      { did: "did:imajin:seller...", amount: 14.37, role: "seller" },
    ]
  }
}
```

---

## 10. Settlement

### Stripe Integration

The pay service creates Stripe Checkout sessions:

```
POST /pay/api/checkout
{
  "items": [{ "name": "Jacket", "amount": 15000, "quantity": 1 }],
  "currency": "CAD",
  "successUrl": "...",
  "cancelUrl": "...",
  "metadata": { "service": "market", "listingId": "lst_abc", "sellerDid": "...", "buyerDid": "..." },
  "fairManifest": { ... },
  "sellerDid": "did:imajin:seller..."
}
→ { "id": "cs_live_xxx", "url": "...", "transactionId": "tx_abc" }
```

Seller DID lookup: if seller has a connected Stripe account (`chargesEnabled: true`), the session routes through Connect with platform fee applied.

### MJNx Ledger

MJN is the internal credit token. 1 MJN = $0.01 (1¢). 100 MJN = 1 MJNx.

Emissions are triggered by the MJN reactor (`packages/bus/src/reactors/mjn.ts`):

```typescript
// EMISSION_SCHEDULE excerpt:
'listing.purchased': {
  gas: 0.01,
  emit: [
    { to: 'subject', amount: '0.25%', reason: 'Purchase reward' },
    { to: 'issuer', amount: '0.25%', reason: 'Sale reward' },
  ],
}
```

The reactor resolves percentage amounts against the settlement value and POSTs credits to pay service `/api/emission`:
```typescript
{
  to_did: targetDid,
  amount: 2.5,               // MJN
  currency: 'MJN',
  reason: 'Purchase reward',
  metadata: { attestation_type: 'listing.purchased', ... }
}
```

### Settlement Schemes

Supported schemes: `x402 | stripe-link | mjnx-direct | solana-pay | lightning`

Configured per manifest in `settlement.schemes`.

---

## 11. Chat

DID-based messaging. Every conversation is a DID.

### Conversation DIDs

```typescript
import { dmDid, groupDid, parseConversationDid } from '@/src/lib/chat/conversation-did';

const did = dmDid(didA, didB);     // deterministic, sorted
const gdid = groupDid([didA, didB, didC]); // deterministic, sorted

parseConversationDid('did:imajin:dm:abc123');
// → { type: 'dm', slug: 'abc123' }
```

### REST API

```
POST /chat/api/d/{conversationDid}/messages
Cookie: imajin_session=...
{
  "content": { "text": "Hello!" },
  "contentType": "text",
  "replyToMessageId": "msg_abc"
}
→ { message: { id, conversationDid, fromDid, content, contentType, signature, createdAt, reactions: [] } }
```

```
GET /chat/api/d/{conversationDid}/messages?limit=50&before=msg_abc
→ { messages: [...], hasMore: true }
```

Requires tier != `soft`. Access verified via auth service.

### Message Types

```typescript
export type MessageContent =
  | { type?: "text"; text: string; encrypted?: string; nonce?: string; meta?: MessageMeta }
  | { type: "voice"; assetId: string; transcript: string; durationMs: number; waveform?: number[] }
  | { type: "media"; assetId: string; filename: string; mimeType: string; size: number; width?: number; height?: number; caption?: string }
  | { type: "location"; lat: number; lng: number; label?: string; accuracy?: number }
  | { type: "system"; event: "member_added" | "member_removed" | "member_left" | "created" | "renamed"; actorDid: string; targetDid?: string; occurredAt?: string; attestationId?: string };
```

### WebSocket Protocol

Connect to `wss://chat.imajin.ai/ws`.

**Authentication:**
- Cookie-based (automatic): session cookie on upgrade request
- Token-based (deferred): connect without cookie, receive `{ type: "auth_required" }`, send `{ type: "auth", token: "..." }`

WS tokens are short-lived (30 seconds, one-time use):
```
POST /chat/api/ws-token
Cookie: imajin_session=...
→ { token: "wst_xxx" }
```

**Client → Server:**
```json
{ "type": "subscribe", "conversationId": "did:imajin:dm:abc123" }
{ "type": "typing", "conversationId": "...", "name": "Alice" }
{ "type": "stop_typing", "conversationId": "..." }
{ "type": "ping" }
```

**Server → Client:**
```json
{ "type": "connected" }
{ "type": "new_message", "message": { ... } }
{ "type": "message_updated", "message": { ... } }
{ "type": "user_typing", "conversationId": "...", "did": "...", "name": "Bob" }
{ "type": "user_presence", "did": "...", "online": true, "lastSeen": null }
{ "type": "pong" }
```

Presence tracked per-DID (multiple tabs share a DID; last tab closing triggers offline).

### E2EE (Simplified)

Chat uses X25519 key agreement + XChaCha20-Poly1305:

```typescript
import {
  generateKeyPair, deriveSharedSecret,
  encryptMessage, decryptMessage
} from '@/src/lib/chat/crypto';

const kp = generateKeyPair();
const shared = deriveSharedSecret(privateKey, peerPublicKey);
const { ciphertext, nonce } = encryptMessage(plaintext, shared);
const plaintext = decryptMessage(ciphertext, nonce, shared);
```

Group chats use a shared group key re-encrypted to each member's X25519 public key.

---

## 12. Connections & Trust Graph

Pods are the unit of trust graph membership. Being in any 2-person pod means you're in the trust graph.

### Schema

```typescript
// From packages/trust-graph/src/schema.ts
export const pods = connectionsSchema.table('pods', {
  id: text('id').primaryKey(),
  name: text('name').notNull(),
  description: text('description'),
  avatar: text('avatar'),
  ownerDid: text('owner_did').notNull(),
  type: text('type', { enum: ['personal', 'shared', 'event'] }).notNull().default('personal'),
  visibility: text('visibility', { enum: ['private', 'trust-bound'] }).notNull().default('private'),
  conversationDid: text('conversation_did'),
  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
  updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
});

export const podMembers = connectionsSchema.table('pod_members', {
  podId: text('pod_id').notNull(),
  did: text('did').notNull(),
  role: text('role', { enum: ['owner', 'admin', 'member'] }).notNull().default('member'),
  addedBy: text('added_by'),
  joinedAt: timestamp('joined_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
  removedAt: timestamp('removed_at'),
});

export const connections = connectionsSchema.table('connections', {
  didA: text('did_a').notNull(),
  didB: text('did_b').notNull(),
  connectedAt: timestamp('connected_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
  disconnectedAt: timestamp('disconnected_at'),
});
```

### Trust Distance

BFS shortest path in hops between two DIDs through pod co-membership:

```typescript
import { trustDistance, trustRadius, resolvePodMembers } from '@imajin/trust-graph';

const hops = await trustDistance(db, didA, didB, maxHops = 6);
// Returns -1 if no path found

const radius = await trustRadius(db, didA, maxHops = 3);
// Returns Set of all DIDs within N hops

const members = await resolvePodMembers(db, podId, maxDepth = 3);
// Recursively resolves members from linked child pods
```

### Invites

```
POST /connections/api/invites
{ "delivery": "link", "note": "...", "maxUses": 1 }
→ { invite: { id, code, url, ... } }
```

Link invite limits by role: admin (unlimited), legendary (10), trusted (5), member (3), newbie (1).

---

## 13. Agent Identity

Agents are first-class identities with scoped delegation.

### Creating an Agent

```
POST /auth/api/agents
Cookie: imajin_session=...
{ "handle": "my-agent", "name": "My Agent", "displayName": "Helper" }
→ { agent: { did, handle, name, publicKey, tier: 'preliminary' } }
```

This:
1. Generates an Ed25519 keypair for the agent
2. Creates an `identities` row with `subtype: 'agent'`, `scope: 'actor'`
3. Creates an `identity_members` row linking the agent to the caller with role `agent`

### Listing Agents

```
GET /auth/api/agents
→ { agents: [{ did, handle, displayName, name, tier, status, role }] }
```

### OpenClaw Plugin Integration

Agents authenticate via the same challenge-response flow as humans. The OpenClaw Imajin channel plugin (`extensions/imajin`) uses:

1. **Agent DID** — stored in plugin config
2. **Challenge-response** — agent signs challenges with its Ed25519 key
3. **X-Acting-For header** — after auth, the agent includes this header to act on behalf of its principal

### Agent Challenge-Response

Same as human challenge-response, but the agent's DID is used:
```
POST /auth/api/challenge
{ "did": "did:imajin:agent_xxx" }
→ { challengeId, challenge, expiresAt }

POST /auth/api/authenticate
{ "challengeId": "...", "signature": "..." }
```

---

## 14. DFOS Integration

### Relay

DFOS relay runs at `registry.imajin.ai/relay/`. Every node runs an embedded relay.

### Bridge

The `@imajin/dfos` package bridges Imajin Ed25519 keypairs to DFOS identity chains:

```typescript
import { createIdentityChain, updateIdentityChain, verifyChain } from '@imajin/dfos';

// Create chain
const { did, log, operationCID } = await createIdentityChain({ privateKeyHex, publicKeyHex });

// Verify chain
const verified = await verifyChain(log);
// → { did, authKeys: [...], assertKeys: [...], controllerKeys: [...], createdAt, updatedAt }
```

### Content Publishing

```typescript
import { publishContentEvent, getContentEvent } from '@imajin/dfos';

const event = await publishContentEvent({
  privateKeyHex,
  publicKeyHex,
  manifest: fairManifest,
  contentCid: 'bafyrei...',
});
```

---

## 15. Integration Patterns

### Adding a New Userspace App

Every new app follows the same pattern:

```typescript
import { requireAuth, requireHardDID, requireAppAuth } from '@imajin/auth';
import { publish } from '@imajin/bus';

// 1. Auth guard
const authResult = await requireAuth(request, { service: 'myapp' });
if ('error' in authResult) { return Response.json({ error: ... }, { status: ... }); }

// 2. Emit business event
await publish('myapp.action', {
  issuer: identity.id,
  subject: targetDid,
  scope: 'myapp',
  payload: { context_id: resourceId, context_type: 'myapp', ... }
});
```

### Environment Variables

```
AUTH_SERVICE_URL=https://jin.imajin.ai/auth   (kernel path, not separate service)
PAY_SERVICE_URL=https://jin.imajin.ai/pay
CHAT_SERVICE_URL=https://jin.imajin.ai/chat
ATTESTATION_INTERNAL_API_KEY=...
AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY=64-char-hex...
NODE_DID=did:imajin:node_xxx
```

### Cross-Service Calls

Forward the session cookie:
```typescript
const response = await fetch(`${CHAT_SERVICE_URL}/api/d/${did}/messages`, {
  headers: { Cookie: request.headers.get('cookie') || '' }
});
```

### Database Pattern

Each service defines its schema in `src/db/schema.ts` using Drizzle ORM. The kernel uses per-service schemas:

```typescript
import { pgSchema } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
export const eventsSchema = pgSchema('events');
export const marketSchema = pgSchema('market');
```

Shared DB connection via `@imajin/db`:
```typescript
import { createDb } from '@imajin/db';
const db = createDb(schema);
```

---

## 16. Cryptographic Stack

| Primitive | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| Ed25519 | DID keypairs, attestation signing, .fair signing, session auth |
| SHA-256 | Content hashing, hard DID derivation |
| X25519 | Chat E2EE key agreement (derived from Ed25519) |
| XChaCha20-Poly1305 | Chat message encryption |
| XSalsa20-Poly1305 | Vault encryption (NaCl box) |
| dag-cbor + CID | DFOS content addressing, attestation CIDs |
| JWS | DFOS signature format for chain operations |
| JWT | Session cookies (HS256, signed by auth service) |

Key encodings:
- Ed25519 private key: 32-byte seed, hex-encoded (64 chars) or PKCS#8 DER (96 chars)
- Ed25519 public key: 32-byte point, hex-encoded (64 chars)
- Ed25519 signature: 64 bytes, hex-encoded (128 chars)
- DFOS keys also in multibase (`z` prefix, base58btc)

The same Ed25519 keypair produces both `did:imajin:xxx` and `did:dfos:xxx`. Byte-compatible. Also a valid Solana wallet address.

---

## 17. Quick Reference

- Source: github.com/ima-jin/imajin-ai
- Kernel: jin.imajin.ai (consolidated auth/pay/chat/media/profile/connections/registry)
- DFOS relay: registry.imajin.ai/relay/
- OpenAPI specs: https://jin.imajin.ai/api/spec (kernel), https://{app}.imajin.ai/api/spec (userspace)

### Common curl Flows

```bash
# 1. Soft DID session
curl -c cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/session/soft \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"email":"user@example.com","name":"Jane"}'

# 2. Get session
curl -b cookies.txt https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/session

# 3. Challenge for keypair auth
curl -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/challenge \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"handle":"alice"}'

# 4. Authenticate with signature
curl -c cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/authenticate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"challengeId":"chl_xxx","signature":"deadbeef..."}'

# 5. Create attestation
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/attestations \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"issuer_did":"did:imajin:...","subject_did":"did:imajin:...","type":"vouch","signature":"..."}'

# 6. Send message
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST "https://jin.imajin.ai/chat/api/d/did:imajin:dm:abc123/messages" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"content":{"text":"Hello!"},"contentType":"text"}'

# 7. Get WS token
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/chat/api/ws-token
→ { "token": "wst_xxx" }

# 8. Create listing (userspace: market app)
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://market.imajin.ai/api/listings \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title":"Jacket","price":15000,"currency":"CAD","sellerTier":"public_onplatform"}'

# 9. Create invite
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/connections/api/invites \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"delivery":"link"}'
```

---

# END OF DOCUMENT
